extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin is a laboratory incubator built as an insulated styrofoam box (volume 0.03 m³) equipped with a heatbed, a 0.02 m³/s fan for air circulation, and three DS18S20 high‑precision 1‑Wire temperature sensors (±0.5 °C accuracy from –10 °C to +85 °C). The control electronics are a Raspberry Pi 4 Model B that drives the heatbed via MOSFETs isolated by optocouplers, and interfaces with the sensors and fan through GPIO. The system operates in a biological laboratory environment, maintaining a target temperature range (LL–UL) with a bang‑bang controller that uses heating duration (H) and waiting duration (C). The incubator’s physical processes (thermal inertia of the heatbed, air mixing, sensor feedback) are modeled in two simulation models (Model A with two free parameters, Model B with four free parameters), with Model B providing better prediction of heatbed inertia. The entire hardware and software stack is open‑source and designed for easy deployment and digital twinning.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin offers a suite of services: (1) real‑time monitoring and visualization of incubator status via a time‑series database and dashboard; (2) on‑demand calibration of the thermal model (Model B) using automated calibration routines; (3) anomaly detection and heatbed state estimation through a Kalman filter that fuses measured sensor data with the unmeasured heatbed temperature; (4) what‑if simulation capability that runs co‑simulations with historical or live PT data to optimize controller parameters; (5) a self‑adaptation loop that detects behavioral changes (e.g., new plant load), schedules experiments, re‑calibrates the model, updates the Kalman filter, runs what‑if analyses, and re‑configures the controller. These services target plant operators, process engineers, and researchers needing predictive control, fault detection, and optimization of the incubator’s thermal performance.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The implementation relies on a RabbitMQ message broker running on the Raspberry Pi to coordinate communication between the controller, low‑level driver, and the digital‑twin components. The controller and low‑level driver are software modules written for the Raspberry Pi that interface with the DS18S20 sensors and MOSFET‑driven heatbed via GPIO, with optocouplers providing electrical isolation. The digital‑twin software is open‑source and hosted on GitHub (https://github.com/INTO‑CPS‑Association/example-incubator). Simulation models of the incubator’s thermal dynamics (Model A and Model B) are implemented in software and used for prediction, calibration, and co‑simulation. A Kalman‑filter routine is incorporated for state estimation and anomaly detection. The system uses a time‑series database to store telemetry and a dashboard for visualization, though the specific database and dashboard technologies are not named in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The digital twin operates on a time scale governed by the physical plant’s thermal dynamics and the controller’s bang‑bang logic. The low‑level driver reads temperature sensors periodically (frequency not specified in the documents) and publishes the readings to RabbitMQ. The controller receives these messages, applies a bang‑bang control strategy with heating duration (H) and waiting duration (C) parameters, and publishes actuator commands. The fan’s airflow is 0.02 m³/s in a 0.03 m³ box, implying an air‑mixing time of ≈2 s, which sets a lower bound on the control loop latency. No explicit synchronization frequency or latency requirement is stated, but the system relies on asynchronous message passing via RabbitMQ, which introduces negligible latency on a local network. The digital twin’s temporal granularity is therefore at the level of individual sensor readings and actuator commands, typically on the order of seconds.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The architecture comprises a single physical twin (the incubator) and a single digital twin instance that mirrors its state. The digital twin is centralized: a RabbitMQ server runs on the same Raspberry Pi that hosts the controller. Coordination occurs through message queues: the low‑level driver publishes sensor data and consumes actuator commands; the controller consumes sensor data, publishes control states, and consumes actuator commands. Responsibilities are partitioned as follows: the low‑level driver handles hardware abstraction (DS18S20 sensors, MOSFET‑controlled heatbed, fan), the controller implements the bang‑bang control logic, and the digital twin components (e.g., Kalman filter, calibration routines) subscribe to the same message streams to perform state estimation, anomaly detection, and what‑if simulations.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The incubator digital twin is orchestrated as a set of loosely coupled components that communicate via RabbitMQ running on the Raspberry Pi. Data from the PT is streamed to a time‑series database (e.g., InfluxDB or similar) and visualised in a dashboard. extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The DT comprises two analytical thermal models (Model A and Model B) that predict the temperature inside the insulated incubator box. Model A is a single‑parameter heat‑balance model with two free parameters: C_air = 616.56 J/K and G_box = 0.65 J/K. Model B extends this with four free parameters to capture the heat‑bed inertia: C_air = 486.12 J/K, G_box = 0.856 J/K, C_heater = 33.65 J/K, G_heater = 0.87 J/K. Both models are implemented in the DT as differential‑equation solvers that receive real‑time temperature data from the PT via the low‑level driver. Data are streamed through a RabbitMQ message broker; each temperature reading is published to a topic that the DT subscribes to. Calibration is performed by running a dedicated experiment (Section 3.3) that generates a data trajectory; the DT then runs a parameter‑estimation routine to update the model coefficients. The models are used for simulation, what‑if analysis, and as the basis for a Kalman‑filter‑based state estimator that infers the unmeasured heat‑bed temperature. The DT also stores the raw sensor data and calibrated model parameters in a time‑series database for audit and visualization.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation is performed by a fan and a heat‑bed. The fan is driven by a 12 V or 18 V supply; the controller toggles the fan on/off via a low‑level driver that writes the command to RabbitMQ. The heat‑bed is controlled by a bang‑bang (on/off) heater whose duty cycle is governed by the controller’s state chart (Fig. 8). The controller implements four tunable parameters: lower limit LL, upper limit UL, heating duration H, and waiting duration C. Commands are sent as JSON messages over RabbitMQ; the low‑level driver subscribes to the “control” queue and applies the command to the hardware. Safety constraints are enforced by a safe protocol embedded in the driver: it checks that the fan voltage and heater duty cycle remain within specified bounds and that the PT remains in a safe temperature envelope before executing any command. The addressing mechanism is message‑based: each actuator command includes a target identifier (e.g., “fan”, “heater”) and the driver routes it to the corresponding GPIO or PWM controller on the Raspberry Pi.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Three temperature sensors are deployed: T1 is positioned outside the insulated box to measure ambient room temperature; T2 is placed at the hottest point inside the box; T3 is placed at the coldest point inside the box. The low‑level driver periodically reads all three sensors, publishes the readings to RabbitMQ, and stores them in a time‑series database. The DT computes an average internal temperature by averaging the readings from T2 and T3, which is used for calibration, state estimation, and anomaly detection. The sensors provide real‑time temperature data that feed the analytical models and the Kalman filter, enabling continuous monitoring of the incubator’s thermal state.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model B is calibrated against experimental data from a controlled heating‑cooling cycle, ensuring that the model captures the thermal inertia of the heatbed. Validation is performed by comparing simulated temperature trajectories to the measured sensor data; discrepancies trigger anomaly detection via a Kalman filter that estimates the unmeasured heatbed temperature. When the filter residuals exceed a threshold, the self‑adaptation loop initiates a new calibration experiment, re‑configures the Kalman filter with updated parameters, and re‑optimizes the controller. Safety is guaranteed by the low‑level driver’s safe protocol, which prevents unsafe actuator commands. Data integrity and traceability are maintained through RabbitMQ message logging and persistent storage in a time‑series database, providing a reliable audit trail for model updates and control actions.

extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The incubator DT lifecycle is articulated in several distinct phases:
1. **Conceptual & Physical Twin (PT) Design** – selection of hardware (Raspberry Pi 4, DS18S20 sensors, MOSFET‑based PCB, fan, heatbed, styrofoam enclosure) and construction of the physical incubator.
2. **Digital Twin (DT) Modeling** – development of two thermal models: Model A (2 free parameters) and Model B (4 free parameters). Model B is chosen for its superior prediction of heatbed inertia.
3. **Calibration & Validation** – experimental runs to fit Model B parameters; calibration is performed on‑demand via a dedicated calibration routine.
4. **Deployment & Runtime Integration** – installation of RabbitMQ as the communication broker, implementation of a low‑level driver (sensor/actuator abstraction), and a bang‑bang controller (LL, UL, H, C parameters) on the Raspberry Pi.
5. **Runtime Monitoring & Insight Generation** – real‑time visualization of PT status in a dashboard, anomaly detection, and heatbed state estimation using a Kalman filter that fuses Model B with sensor data.
6. **Self‑Adaptation Loop** – detection of plant behavior change triggers an experiment schedule, re‑calibration of Model B, re‑configuration of the Kalman filter and controller, and what‑if simulations to re‑optimize control parameters.
7. **Evolution & Governance** – iterative refinement of models and algorithms, with all code and data released as open source (GitHub repository). Transition management is handled by calibration updates that replace model parameters and by the Kalman filter’s state updates, ensuring the DT remains aligned with the PT.

Representation types evolve from a purely physical system to a hybrid PT/DT system comprising hardware, simulation models, data streams, and control algorithms, with governance implied by the open‑source release and iterative calibration cycle.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

**Low-level driver** – Abstracts the low level communication with the sensors and actu
ators.

**Controller** – Implements the control logic required to regulate the temperature inside
the incubator. It receives the measured data and sends commands through the
communication server.


**2.2.1** **The Communication Server**


For the case study, the RabbitMQ [4] server was deployed for synchronisation and communication. With tens of thousands of users, RabbitMQ is one of the most popular
open source message brokers. RabbitMQ is a mature, lightweight, and easy to deploy
on premises and in the cloud.

The controller on the Raspberry Pi is listening from the RabbitMQ server for commands of controlling the fan and the heatbed and at the same time sharing the control
states to the RabbitMQ server.
We choose RabbitMQ because it greatly facilitates the digital twinning process, as
the DT can be implemented by components that listen to the messages being passed
between the controller and the load level driver, as illustrated in fig. 7.


**2.2.2** **The Low-Level Driver**


The low-level driver is used to interact with the PT and to issue the states of the PT

to the RabbitMQ server. In order keep the device in a safe state, a safe protocol is
incorporated into the low-level driver.
The low level driver periodically reads the temperature data, and checks if there are
commands from the controller, through the communication server. The temperature
data is uploaded to the communication server.


**2.2.3** **The Controller**


The controller used in the case study is a comparable to a bang-bang controller, which
a small difference described below. The control strategy is executed after receiving the

temperature measurements.
The principle of bang-bang control is that when a reference temperature is given
to the controller, the controller issues a heating signal turning on the heatbed until the
measured temperature from sensors reaches the reference temperature. Because of the
delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to
make sure the temperature does not rise too much. Figure 8 shows the state chart of the
controller. The controller parameters are:

This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary** We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. **2.1.3** **The Fan** We installed a fan (see fig. 3(c)) inside the box to circulate the air inside the box for making the temperature as uniformly distributed as possible. The fan airflow is about 0 _._ 02m [3] s _[−]_ [1] . With a box volume of 0 _._ 03m [3], the fan should take 2 seconds to move all the air within the box. **2.1.4** **The Raspberry Pi** A Raspberry Pi 4 Model B (see fig. 3(d)) was selected to implement the controller. The General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO) connectors on the Raspberry Pi can be used to connect many accessories such as temperature sensors, fan, and the heatbed. Furthermore it supports wireless internet out of the box, with built-in Wi-Fi and Bluetooth, 5 which simplifies deployment of the controller. **2.1.5** **The Temperature Sensor** It is necessary to get feedback from the temperature sensors for feedback control. To do this, a DS18S20 High-Precision 1-Wire Digital Thermometer (see fig. 3(e)) was used for measuring the temperature. This temperature sensor can measure temperatures from -55 °C to +125 °C with an accuracy of 0.5 °C from -10 to +85 °C, which is suitable for the physical incubator. And each DS18S20 has a unique 64-bit serial code, which allows multiple DS18S20s to function on the same 1-Wire bus. Such 1-wire bus support to connect multiple sensors with only one GPIO. In the incubator system, three sensors were deployed for detecting the temperature. **2.1.6** **The PCB Design** Controlling the heatbed requires approximately 4A current which is not supported by the Raspberry Pi. Instead, MOSFETs are used to separate the control signals from the Raspberry Pi and the driving currents. In order to decouple the control signals from the Raspberry Pi to the MOSFETs, we use optocouplers. The PCB schematic is shown in fig. 4. Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

ators.

the incubator. It receives the measured data and sends commands through the
communication server.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.
